# Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Analysis — Deliverables Summary

**Client:** Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.
**Prepared by:** Hartwell & Cabot LLP (Catherine Ng, Partner; Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate)
**Engagement:** Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review (engagement dated April 14, 2025)
**Deliverable date:** May 19, 2025
**Jurisdictions:** New Jersey, New York, Connecticut (current); Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, Washington (expansion)

## Deliverables

### 1. `multi-state-compliance-matrix.xlsx` (8 sheets)
Compares Vantage's current leave policies (Employee Handbook, Rev. Jan 15, 2022) and practices against the statutory/regulatory requirements of all seven jurisdictions, with color-coded gap indicators (red = non-compliant, yellow = at risk/verify, green = compliant). All 7 states appear as columns in every matrix sheet.

- **Cover & Legend** — purpose, sources, legend, reading instructions.
- **Paid Sick Leave** (16 elements) — accrual rate, FT/PT caps, waiting period, carryover, covered uses (own illness/preventive, family care, safe leave/DV-SA-stalking, public health emergency, school-related, bereavement), family-member definition, documentation threshold, payout, posting, recordkeeping.
- **Paid Family Leave** (15 elements) — program/private-plan status, eligibility, duration, wage replacement, weekly cap, qualifying reasons (bonding, caregiving, military exigency, additional reasons), family-member definition, foreseeable/unforeseeable notice, job protection, anti-retaliation, contribution funding.
- **Paid Medical Leave** (5 elements) — availability for own serious health condition, duration, wage replacement/cap, interaction with employer STD (Keystone), employee access/claims routing.
- **Employer Obligations** (8 elements) — registration status/deadlines, employee & employer contribution rates/wage bases, PinnacleHR withholding configuration, recordkeeping, posting/notice, private-plan approval status.
- **Contribution Summary** — estimated annual employee/employer/total contributions per state (NJ $228,830; NY $232,000; CT $144,500; MA ~$68,640; CO ~$45,900; OR ~$62,000; WA ~$33,300; current subtotal $605,330; expansion ~$209,840; grand total ~$815,170), with wage bases and the PinnacleHR capability note.
- **PTO Payout on Separation** (4 elements) — payout requirement, forfeit permissibility, carryover cap, waiting-time penalties.
- **Gap Summary** — consolidated, severity-sorted list of all 263 red/yellow findings (83 Critical, 113 High, 67 Moderate).

### 2. `gap-analysis-memo.docx`
A narrative memorandum identifying all material compliance deficiencies, categorized by severity — **6 Critical, 15 High, 14 Moderate findings** — with legal-risk explanations, three cross-cutting structural analyses, and a prioritized remediation plan of **20 action items** with owners and deadlines tied to the facility-opening schedule (MA 8/1/25, CO 11/1/25, OR 2/1/26, WA 5/1/26) and the July 1, 2025 revised-Handbook target.

**Critical (6):** (1) NJ Earned Sick Leave narrow family-member definition/covered uses — active NJ DOL complaint *Muñoz* (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117), incl. the "own health-related needs"/"to the extent required by law" framing; (2) self-funded PFL structurally non-compliant in all 7 states (below statutory on rate/cap/duration; no private-plan approval; CO no opt-out; incl. the §6.3.7 "intended to satisfy state mandate" conflict and dual-structure confusion); (3) paid medical leave gap with documented HR misdirection to employee-paid Keystone STD (7 inquiries across CT/NJ/NY); (4) MA PFML registration deadline ~June 1, 2025; (5) CO FAMLI registration potentially already overdue (3 April remote hires — ops manager + 2 coordinators; no opt-out); (6) MA PTO payout non-compliance (Wage Act treble damages) before Aug 1, 2025 opening.

**High (15):** sick leave accrual rate (1/40 vs. 1/30 in 5 states); PT cap (24 hrs in all 7); FT cap below statute (NY 56/CO 48/WA no-cap); no-carryover (all 7); 120-day waiting period (5 states); covered-uses/family-member definition (all states, incl. CO HFWA 80-hr supplemental PHE leave); PFL notice (no unforeseeable distinction + 5-business-day response review); PFL qualifying reasons (military exigency/organ donation/safe leave); PFL family-member definition; PFL eligibility; PTO payout CO/OR/WA; OR/CO registration deadlines; manual spreadsheet tracking risk; **PFL AWW calculation methodology** (8-week lookback, base pay only, excludes OT/bonuses/commissions); **PFL intermittent-leave restriction** (full-day increments, supervisor approval, operational-disruption denial).

**Moderate (14):** documentation threshold (2 vs. 3 days) + pattern-of-abuse review; posting/notice; recordkeeping method; NJ/NY PTO payout at risk; PTO carryover cap at risk; bereavement (verify); employee confusion; inconsistent application; outdated handbook + facility-address discrepancies (Newark 1275 Raymond vs 78 Doremus; Queens 56-10 vs 55-20; White Plains 200 Hamilton vs 1200 Westchester; Bridgeport 825 East Main vs 300 Seaview); WA registration timing; waiting-time penalties; key-employee reinstatement exception; Keystone STD extension to expansion states; PinnacleHR configuration dependency (sole blocker is registration).

## Completeness audit
A re-read of all source documents against the deliverables identified and filled 13 gaps: matrix contribution dollar amounts (added Contribution Summary sheet); and in the memo — AWW calculation methodology, intermittent-leave restriction, §6.3.7 "intended to satisfy" language, key-employee exception, PinnacleHR existing-config/sole-blocker note, interim status date (May 2, 2025), CO HFWA 80-hr supplemental leave, sick-leave "own health-related"/"to the extent required by law" framing, facility-address discrepancies, CO remote-hire role detail, 5-business-day PFL approval response, and Keystone STD extension. Both files re-validated (schema-valid). Where the record was silent, items are flagged "verify" rather than guessed.

## Source documents reviewed
Employee Handbook §6 (Rev. Jan 15, 2022); PinnacleHR withholding summary (Apr 10, 2025); NJ DOL complaint & Feb 21, 2025 response (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117); HR expansion memo (Mar 28, 2025); internal HR/GC email thread (Mar 10–17, 2025); leave-tracking sample workbook; engagement letter (Apr 14, 2025).

  **Complaint Received by Employer:**   February 3, 2025
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[Section 1 --- Nature of Complaint]{.underline}**

The above-named Complainant, Carla Muñoz, has filed a complaint with the New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Division of Wage and Hour Compliance, pursuant to the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.* (the \"Act\").

Ms. Muñoz is employed by Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (\"Respondent\" or \"Employer\") as a Warehouse Associate at the Employer\'s Edison, New Jersey facility located at 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, NJ 08817. Complainant alleges that on or about a date in late January 2025, she submitted a request to use accrued earned sick leave for the purpose of caring for her domestic partner\'s child, who was experiencing an illness. At the time of the request, Complainant states she had a sufficient balance of accrued earned sick leave hours available for use.

Complainant alleges that her request was denied by her direct supervisor, who informed her that the Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy permits the use of sick leave only for the care of a \"spouse, child, or parent\" and that a domestic partner\'s child did not qualify as a covered family member under the terms of that policy.

The Act defines \"family member\" broadly under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 to include, among other relationships, a child of a domestic partner, a domestic partner or civil union partner of the employee, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship. The Employer\'s policy language limiting covered family members to \"spouse, child, or parent\" is narrower than the statutory definition and, as applied in this instance, excluded a relationship --- the child of a domestic partner --- that is expressly covered by the Act.

As a result of the denial, Complainant states she was required to take unpaid time off or use accrued paid time off (PTO) from a separate leave bank in order to care for the child. Complainant asserts that the denial constituted an improper restriction on her right to use earned sick leave for a covered purpose under the Act, namely the care of a family member during illness, which is a use expressly permitted by N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3.

**[Section 2 --- Statutory Basis and Alleged Violations]{.underline}**

This complaint is brought under the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*, which became effective October 29, 2018. The Act requires every employer in the State of New Jersey to provide earned sick leave to employees and to permit the use of such leave for specified purposes.

**Alleged Violation 1 --- Improperly Restrictive Definition of \"Family Member\"**

The Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy defines covered family members as \"spouse, child, or parent.\" This definition excludes domestic partners, children of domestic partners, civil union partners, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, other blood relatives, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship --- all of whom are included within the definition of \"family member\" under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1. By maintaining and applying this narrower definition, the Employer denied Ms. Muñoz the use of accrued earned sick leave for the care of her domestic partner\'s child, a covered family member under the Act.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

## Deliverables Produced

### `multi-state-compliance-matrix.xlsx`
A 7-sheet workbook comparing Vantage's current leave policies (Handbook Rev. Jan 15, 2022) and practices against the statutory requirements of all seven jurisdictions (NJ, NY, CT current; MA, CO, OR, WA expansion), with **red/yellow/green color-coded gap indicators** on every cell:

- **Cover & Legend** + 5 dimension matrices (Paid Sick Leave — 16 elements; Paid Family Leave — 15; Paid Medical Leave — 5; Employer Obligations — 8; PTO Payout on Separation — 4) + a consolidated **Gap Summary** listing all 263 red/yellow findings sorted by severity (83 Critical, 113 High, 67 Moderate).
- Covers every dimension the engagement letter specified: accrual rates, caps, waiting periods, carryover, covered uses (including DV/SA/stalking, public health emergencies, preventive care, bereavement), family-member definitions, documentation thresholds, posting/notice, eligibility, duration, wage replacement/caps, qualifying reasons (bonding/caregiving/military exigency), notice (foreseeable/unforeseeable), job protection, registration deadlines, contribution rates/wage bases, withholding, recordkeeping, private-plan status, and PTO payout/forfeit/waiting-time penalties.

### `gap-analysis-memo.docx`
A narrative memorandum from H&C to Vantage's GC categorizing every deficiency by severity (6 Critical, 13 High, 11 Moderate), with legal-risk explanations, three cross-cutting structural analyses (self-funded plan restructuring, manual-tracking replacement, NJ DOL complaint posture), and a **prioritized remediation plan of 19 action items** with owners and deadlines tied to the facility-opening schedule (MA 8/1/25, CO 11/1/25, OR 2/1/26, WA 5/1/26) and the July 1, 2025 revised-Handbook target.

## Key findings surfaced
- **Active exposure:** the NJ DOL *Muñoz* complaint (narrow family-member definition/covered uses) is a leading indicator of a systemic 7-state defect.
- **Structural failure:** the self-funded PFL (50%/$850/8 wks) is below statutory minimums in all 7 states, no private-plan approval filed anywhere, and CO permits no opt-out.
- **Paid medical leave gap:** documented HR misdirection of employees to employee-paid Keystone STD instead of the state programs they fund.
- **Imminent deadlines:** MA PFML registration by ~June 1, 2025; CO FAMLI likely already overdue (3 April remote hires).
